observations.recommendation:
1.23 To ensure the accuracy and reliability of PPE balances and compliance with
COA Circular No. 2020-006 and IPSAS 1, we recommended and
Management agreed to:

a. Instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct a full inventory-taking of all
PPE, including those acquired through the Special Education Fund (SEF),
and update the Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and
Equipment (RPCPPE) using the ICF as a reference document.

b. Require the Inventory Committee to itemize all PPE and specify its
location, property number, and relevant details to enhance accountability
and verifiability.

c. Instruct the Property Unit to complete all required information in the
RPCPPE, including balance per card and per count, property numbers,
and units of measure, ensuring compliance with prescribed formats.

d. Instruct the MTO to regularly update Property Cards (PC) for all PPE
and ensure the issuance of Property Acknowledgment Receipts (PAR) to
end-users, replacing outdated Acknowledgment Receipts for Equipment
(ARE).

e. Expedite the creation of a Disposal and Appraisal Committee to assess and
recommend disposal actions, ensure unserviceable and obsolete items are
properly marked in the RPCPPE and reconciled with the IIRUP, and
promptly dispose of these assets to prevent further depreciation and
optimize storage space.

f. Require the Property Unit to regularly reconcile RPCPPE balances with
accounting records, ensuring accurate reflection of asset conditions and
usability in financial statements.

2.11 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, as GSO-Designee, maintain
Local Road Network Property Cards for all roads and their components, and
a Local Road Inventory and Road Map. We also recommended that an
inventory be conducted each year and the corresponding Report on the
Physical Count of the Road Network System be prepared and submitted to
the Auditor as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-008.

2.12 We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain Local
Road Network Ledger Cards (LRNLC) with complete descriptions and
reconcile the same with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map to ensure
completeness, as well as to recognize depreciation of the depreciable
components thereof and provide disclosures on the account in the Notes to
Financial Statements.

3.9 We recommended that the Municipal Engineer coordinate closely with the
Municipal Accountant by submitting reports of completed projects, along
with copies of Certificates of Completion and Acceptance and related
Program of Work at least on a quarterly basis so that timely and accurate
recording of the completed projects could be made in the books of accounts.

3.10 We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer ensure that the GPPA
report is complete, accurate, and consistent with the financial records
maintained by the Municipal Accountant and Budget Officer.

3.11 Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough
review of the CIP Accounts to identify completed projects and prepare the
necessary Journal Entry Vouchers to transfer these projects from the CIP
account to the appropriate PPE accounts, adjust any erroneous entries, and
compute and record the applicable depreciation expenses to reflect the
current value of the assets.

4.5 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant carefully review the
transactions and make necessary adjustments to ensure that transactions are
accurately recorded in the correct account.

5.9 We recommended and Management agreed to stop the practice of granting
cash advances to employees not designated as Disbursing Officers in
compliance with COA Circular 97-002.

5.10 We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to refrain from
approving cash advances to employees who are not designated as Disbursing
Officers unless a copy of their designation as Special Disbursing Officers is
submitted.

5.11 We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to require
these Accountable Officers to immediately submit the Liquidation Reports
and pertinent supporting documents for the outstanding Cash Advances.

6.6 We recommended and Management agreed to:
(a) Require all concerned accountable officers to immediately settle their
cash advances in accordance with the pertinent sections of COA Circular
No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997;
(b) Require the preparation of a reimbursement voucher where the amount
of cash advance is less than the traveling expenses incurred, and
recognize the cash advance as liquidated;
(c) Direct the Municipal Accountant to monitor and strictly enforce the
liquidation of cash advances within the prescribed period and see to it
that no additional cash advances are granted unless the previous ones are
settled or properly accounted for;
(d) Direct the Municipal Accountant to record the liquidation within 10 days
from receipt of the Liquidation Report from the Accountable Officer
before submitting the same to the Office of the Auditor for issuance of a
Credit Notice, and
(e) Instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct regular
evaluation/validation of all outstanding accounts, to send demand letters
to officers and employees with long outstanding accounts, and to impose
sanctions when necessary, such as withholding of their salaries as
provided in the Circular.

7.7 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require all recipients of the
disbursed funds to submit complete documentation supporting the utilization
of the amounts received. This includes, but is not limited to, Memoranda of
Agreement (MOA), Acknowledgment Receipts, and other supporting
documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001.

7.8 We further recommended that Management discontinue the practice of
releasing funds as outright financial assistance to school representatives, SB
Members, or unauthorized employees for participation in cultural activities.
Instead, any future disbursements related to such programs should strictly
adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002.

7.9 Lastly, we recommended that Management refrain from directly charging
disbursed amounts to expense accounts without requiring proper
documentation and liquidation.

8.10 We recommended that Management replenish the portion of the 20% DF
that was inappropriately disbursed by appropriating from the General Fund,
and henceforth, comply with the guidelines set forth in the DILG and DBM
JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017, as well as the limitations on the
use of the 20% Development Fund outlined under the DBM, DOF, and DILG
JMC No. 1, s. 2020 dated November 4, 2020.

9.8 We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Cease the charging of LDRRMF for expenditures not aligned with the
four thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management. Instead,
ensure that the regular operational needs of the MDRRMO and similar
offices are adequately funded through the General Fund or other
appropriate budgetary allocations.
b. Require the submission of complete supporting documents such as
attendance sheets and duly accomplished Inspection and Acceptance
Reports (IARs) for all disbursed expenses, particularly those related to
the Araw nang Bayan celebration, to validate claims and ensure
accountability.

10.13 We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer, remit intact all taxes withheld from compensation and
payments for purchases and from LGU-collected Documentary Stamp Tax
in accordance with the prescribed periods outlined in BIR Revenue
Memorandum Circular (RMC) No. 23-2012, dated February 14, 2012.

10.14 Additionally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that
the correct amount of annual taxes due on compensation be computed and
remitted in compliance with the provisions of Revenue Regulation 2- 98.

11.12 We recommended that Management implement a review process that would
ensure that the monthly remittance matches the total funds withheld,
including the coordination with GSIS, HDMF, and PHIC for the conduct of
reconciliations to address any discrepancies between the withheld amounts
and the prepared remittances and thereafter ensure the timely remittance of
all withheld funds in compliance with the applicable laws, rules, and
regulations governing the remittances to GSIS, PhilHealth, and PAG-IBIG.

We recommended and Management agreed to enjoin the Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough review and analysis of the recorded A/Ps amounting to ₱3,675,362.75 as of December 31, 2023, and recommend for reversion all those which remained outstanding for two years or more, which no actual claims, administrative or judicial, have been filed or which are not covered by perfected contracts on record in accordance with Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445 and DBM-COA Joint Circular No. 99- 06 dated November 13, 1999.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to observe the guidelines and
procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing
of PPE account balances to have reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to
existence, condition, and accountability as follows:

a. Draw journal entries to record the restoration of the missing and
unserviceable properties incorrectly dropped from the books;

b. Submit the approved PIP to the COA Audit Team within the prescribed
period;

c. Conduct the required preliminary activities before inventory taking; and

d. Strictly follow the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of physical count
of PPE, Recognition of PPE items found at the station, and disposition for
non-existing/missing PPE items and require the Accountant and Property
Officer to reconcile their records based on the results of the actual physical
inventory and effect necessary adjustments.

We recommended and Management agreed to enjoin the Municipal Accountant
to conduct a thorough review and analysis of the recorded A/Ps amounting to
₱3,675,362.75 as of December 31, 2023, and recommend for reversion all those
which remained outstanding for two years or more, and which no actual claims,
administrative or judicial, have been filed or which are not covered by perfected
contracts on record in accordance with Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445 and DBM-
COA Joint Circular No. 99- 06 dated November 13, 1999.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. Terminate the time deposits in the Trust Funds and ensure that only idle
funds in the General Fund are placed in time deposit accounts pursuant to
Section 21 of COA Circular No. 92-382; and

b. Ensure that funds are readily available for the implementation of risk
reduction and management activities and priority projects for the immediate
benefit of its constituents. The implementation of priority
programs/projects/activities should not be delayed or sacrificed in favor of
earning interest in a time deposit.

We recommended that Management submit the lacking documents as required
under Section 13.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to enable us to properly
evaluate the propriety of the payment.

We further recommended that, henceforth, the Municipal Accountant ensure
the completeness of the supporting documents for every transaction of the
Municipality before payment is made in compliance with Section 4.6 of PD No.
1445.

Committee to:

IRR of R.A. No. 9184 if available, otherwise, provide an explanation for
the non-preparation of the said documents; and

No. 9184 regarding the conduct of public bidding.

001, and closely monitor its completion; and

appropriate funds for the computerization of the assessment records to

in identifying the PPAs to be charged to the previous years’ unexpended
LDRRMF balances for inclusion in the LDRRMFIP in accordance with Section
5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

6. We recommended and Management agreed to seek the concurrence of the Commission on Audit and secure a clearance from the Solicitor General prior to the hiring of private lawyers as legal counsels, pursuant to COA Circular No. 95-011 dated December 4, 1995, as amended, and further provided under COA Circular No. 2021-003 dated July 16, 2021, to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary expenditures.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

8. We recommended that the SWO III and the Municipal Engineer coordinate with the Punong Barangays for the submission of the list of recipient-beneficiaries duly acknowledging receipt of construction materials as well as documents validating the qualification of beneficiaries.

Implemented
Implemented

observations.managementAction: Implemented

10. We recommended that the Municipal DRRM Officer properly identify and assess the hazards susceptibility and the related risk that may occur in the Municipality in order to formulate a feasible LDRRMFIP.
11. We further recommended that the Municipal DRRM Officer strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the goals and objectives of the fund as well as the desired benefits and strictly observe the provisions of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2013-1 in allocating LDRRM Fund solely for the implementation of DRRM-related projects.
observations.managementAction:
Implemented
Implemented

observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.managementAction: Not Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to: a) Stop the practice of recording purchases of supplies and materials directly to the related expense account and, henceforth, record purchases and utilization of these items in accordance with Sections 114 and 121 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I; b) Require the requisitioning office needing the supplies and materials to prepare and submit the RIS to the GSO on a weekly basis; c) Require the GSO to consolidate all the RIS on the SSMI and submit the same to the Accounting Office; and d) Require the Provincial Accountant to record to the related expense accounts the supplies consumed based on the SSMI and to maintain perpetual inventory records to be reconciled periodically with the records of the GSO in order to fairly present the expense and inventory accounts in the financial statements.
observations.reason: Procurement of welfare goods, personal protective supplies, and medical/laboratory supplies were recorded as an outright expense because these were issued directly to the requisitioning office responsible for giving the items to the end-users.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Accountant verify and reconcile the difference between the reciprocal accounts and effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts.

As of December 31, 2021, the unreconciled balance amounted to ₱3,523,575.59.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor, in coordination with the Appraisal and Disposal Committee, give priority in the disposal of the unserviceable properties in accordance with Section 79 of PD 1445. Henceforth, after disposal, the General Service Officer shall submit the necessary documents together with the Official Receipt to the Accounting Office as basis for the latter to drop the unserviceable properties from PPE accounts for proper presentation in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: CY 2018 The Appraisal and Disposal Committee had already appraised the value of the unserviceable properties.
